event: 66 years old
male
with a medical history significant for hypertension
hyperlipidemia
hepatitis C virus infection
and atrial fibrillation chronically anticoagulated with apixaban
brought in by emergency services after a fall
reported dizziness
then lost consciousness
was found down by a family member
Primary trauma survey was negative with a Glasgow Coma Score of 15 no focal neurological deficits
Noncontrast CT of the head was significant for a 6 mm right frontal SDH with no shift
right medial orbital wall fracture
had no operative intervention recommended
developed severe headache
head CT was repeated which showed worsening of the SDH with associated mass effect and 2 mm midline shift
remained neurologically intact
was discharged to acute inpatient rehabilitation on the 6th hospital day
experienced a terrible headache for 3 days
underwent burr holes for evacuation of the SDH and placement of subdural drain
following hematoma evacuation, the patient’s headache improved
the SDH decreased in size on repeat CT head
was discharged home on the 2nd postoperative day
described a strange sensation in the right arm
the feeling that he could not control the arm
was sent directly to the emergency room for further evaluation
developed a focal left arm seizure
was treated for this seizure and started on electroencephalography (EEG) monitoring
was then admitted
was found to be unresponsive
CT head showed no acute hemorrhage
EEG indicated status epilepticus
was intubated for airway protection
transferred to the medical intensive care unit for further management
cerebral CTA was obtained
studies were negative for any pathology
this admission was complicated by ventilator-dependent respiratory failure
septic shock secondary to Pseudomonas bacteremia
received a tracheostomy and gastrostomy
was eventually stabilized and discharged to long-term acute care hospital on day 29
four days after discharge, the patient was found to be less responsive
CT and CTA head showed right frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage measuring 5.2 cm×3.3 cm
cerebral catheter angiography was performed
showed prominence of the anterior temporal branch of the right middle cerebral artery
with early, rapid, shunting of blood through a cortical vein to the superior sagittal sinus
opacification of a capillary-like serpiginous tangle of vessels connecting the anterior temporal artery with the early draining vein, representing an AVM
Grade 1 (Spetzler-Ponce Grade A)
on the second postangiogram day, embolization of the AVM was performed
using N-butyl- cyanoacrylate glue
the embolization was successful
no opacification of the early draining vein or AVM nidus were seen
on the 8th day postembolization, the patient was taken to the operating room for resection of the AVM
right pterional craniotomy was performed
was separated from surrounding brain parenchyma using microdissection techniques
feeding arteries, followed by the draining vein, were coagulated using bipolar electrocautery and resected
following resection, intraoperative Doppler ultrasound indicated no arterial flow in the draining vein
the associated intraparenchymal hematoma was also evacuated
postoperative angiography showed no residual AVM
postoperatively, the patient’s mental status progressively improved
was complicated by pseudomonas sepsis
eventually discharged to long-term acute care on the 15th postoperative day
